metadata.summary: Peer-benchmark table methodology: classify 8-15 comparables into five revenue archetypes, extract P25/P50/P75 distributions, overlay founder plan against distribution.

content.title: Business Model Research (Market-Researcher Lens)
content.summary: Business model research at the market-researcher level produces a peer-benchmark table — not a single canvas. The methodology classifies 8-15 comparables (including failed and acquihired peers) into five revenue archetypes (subscription, one-time, transaction, advertising, marketplace), extracts P25/P50/P75 distributions for ARPU, gross margin, gross logo retention, NDR, and CAC payback, then overlays the founder's plan against the distribution. The core rule: always include at least 2 dead or acquihired comparables — without them, medians overstate viability by 20-40%.

why.p: Survivorship bias in comp sets is the primary failure mode: public-listed comps run higher gross margin (78-82%) than venture-stage privates (60-70%) because of scale. "Tiered subscription" is the default LLM answer for any SaaS, regardless of economics. NDR greater than 110% can hide gross logo churn greater than 25%. A distribution-first approach (P25/P50/P75 per archetype) prevents single-comp dominance and false precision, and explicitly separates the market-researcher deliverable (distributions) from the researcher sibling deliverable (a single canvas).

list.item: Pre-spec market-side answer to "what model do peers in this category actually use?"
Investor or board memo: produce an industry revenue-model distribution by archetype
Pricing committee input: pull ARPU, gross margin, NDR, and rule-of-40 from a public-comp set
Category entry decision: rank 5-15 candidate categories by median LTV:CAC and CAC payback
M&A scoping: build a comps table mapping the target's model to nearest public proxy
Cross-checking a researcher-mode canvas — verify the founder's chosen archetype against industry base rates
Single-product canvas and unit-economics design — that is the researcher/business-model-research sibling's scope
Markets with fewer than 3 public or well-documented private comparables — table is statistically meaningless
Hyper-local services where public comps do not transfer — use local-market survey instead
Pre-revenue categories with no precedent — peer benchmarking is misleading; use analogous-markets or first-principles-pricing
Regulated verticals where revenue model is dictated by law — research the regulation, not the comps

rule.statement: Build the comp universe before classifying the founder's idea. Anchoring on the founder's preferred archetype biases the universe selection and produces a confirmation table, not a benchmark.
rule.rationale: If you know the founder wants "subscription" before pulling comps, you unconsciously include more subscription companies. The benchmark must be built blind to the preferred conclusion.

rule.statement: Always include at least 2 dead or acquihired comparables in the comp set. Without them, medians overstate viability by 20-40%.
rule.rationale: Survivorship bias: the public-listed and well-documented companies are the ones that succeeded. The failures and acquihires that define the real distribution are invisible unless explicitly sought.

list.item: Minimum 8 comparables; downgrade from "benchmark" to "case studies" if fewer than 8 are available
Mix required: public (S-1/10-K), late-stage private (Crunchbase), acquired (M&A press), dead (Crunchbase "Closed")
At least 20% private and at least 10% dead or acquihired
Cap any single comp's weight at 1/N — one dominant peer in a 5-comp set is no longer a benchmark
Cohort by stage (seed/Series-B/listed) AND geography (US/EU/RoW) before computing medians
subscription: more than 60% of revenue from recurring payments
one-time: more than 60% from non-recurring purchases
transaction: more than 60% from per-transaction fees (including marketplace commission)
advertising: more than 60% from ad inventory or sponsorships
marketplace: commission + payments + seller subscriptions + ad fees combined (all-in take rate)
hybrid: no single stream above 60%; tag with the two dominant streams and their split

rule.statement: Never collapse a hybrid model into a single archetype. Shopify is not "subscription" — it is subscription + payments + advertising. Tag hybrid + split, or the archetype table lies.
rule.rationale: Collapsing hybrid revenue models produces incorrect ARPU and gross margin medians. A "subscription" comp that is actually 40% subscription + 40% payments will distort the subscription archetype's metrics.

list.item: ARR: annualized recurring revenue from subscription contracts only
ARPU: ARR divided by total active paying accounts
ACV: annual contract value for a single customer (can differ from ARPU if multi-year discounts apply)
Gross margin: (revenue - COGS) / revenue; excludes operating expenses
Gross logo retention: percentage of customer accounts retained year-over-year regardless of expansion or contraction
NDR (Net Dollar Retention): revenue retained + expansion - contraction - churn from the prior-year cohort

rule.statement: Always pair NDR with gross logo retention. NDR above 110% can coexist with gross logo churn above 25% in a land-and-expand model. Reporting NDR alone hides the real churn problem.
rule.rationale: NDR is a vanity metric when reported in isolation. A small base of large expanding accounts drives NDR up while the majority of smaller accounts churn — the pattern that precedes a retention crisis.

rule.statement: Tag every metric with source_url and filing_period (YYYY-Qn). Benchmarks without provenance cannot be reviewed, challenged, or refreshed.
rule.rationale: Metrics cited without filing dates become unverifiable over time. Partners and product managers need to know whether a comp's metrics are from last quarter or three years ago.

list.item: S-1 and 10-K filings: gold standard for ARR, gross margin, gross logo retention, NDR, CAC payback — use SEC EDGAR full-text search
Wayback Machine pricing snapshots at filing date: list price verified at the moment the metrics were reported
G2 reviewer-disclosed spend: effective pricing from actual buyers, with contract renewal context
Crunchbase + press: funding stage, headcount, and M&A status for private comps
ChartMogul, Baremetrics, ProfitWell public benchmark datasets: pre-computed medians by ARPU band

rule.statement: Convert all non-USD currency at the filing-period FX rate, not today's rate. Record the FX rate used in the comps CSV.
rule.rationale: Currency moves of 10-20% per year between major pairs (EUR/USD, GBP/USD) distort historical comparisons if today's rate is applied retroactively to prior-period filings.

rule.statement: Strip one-time line items (professional services, implementation fees, hardware) from ARR. Include them in a separate "services revenue" column if material (above 10% of total revenue).
rule.rationale: One-time revenue mixed into ARR overstates recurring revenue by 10-25% for companies with implementation-heavy sales motions, making their subscription margins appear higher than reality.

rule.statement: Annualize monthly numbers. Never compare a monthly ARPU from one comp against an annual ACV from another without explicit normalization.
rule.rationale: Period mismatch is the most common metric error in comp tables. $100/month ARPU is $1,200 ACV, not $100 — a 12x difference that distorts tier-mix and LTV/CAC comparisons.

rule.statement: Use Wayback Machine to pin pricing pages to the filing date for each comp. A pricing page scrape newer than the filing period quotes a different price than what the comp's financials reflect.
rule.rationale: SaaS pricing changes frequently (often annually). Using today's pricing against last year's metrics produces false unit-economics comparisons because the price that generated those metrics was different.

rule.statement: Quote distribution (P25/P50/P75), never a single median. Single medians create false precision and hide the strategic range the founder needs to understand.
rule.rationale: The spread between P25 and P75 is the actionable signal. A P25-P75 ARPU spread of $15-$80 in a category tells the founder the model has not standardized. A $40-$50 spread tells them it has.

list.item: Compute P25/P50/P75 per archetype AND per stage (seed/Series-B/listed)
Flag comps more than 2 standard deviations from the median with a footnote explaining the cause (e.g., compute COGS, hardware revenue, geographic discount)
Annotate trends: last 4-8 quarters delta per metric (e.g., usage-based share moved from 20% to 38% of new ARR in dev-tools cohort 2022-2025)
Flag any P50 metric that shifts more than 10% quarter-over-quarter as a "regime change" warrant for the trend-analysis methodology
Green: founder's planned metric within P25-P75 for their archetype and stage
Yellow: founder's metric within P10-P25 or P75-P90 — flagged as ambitious or conservative
Red: founder's metric outside P10-P90 — requires explicit justification or model revision

rule.statement: Do not rewrite the founder's plan. Emit a delta only — which cells are out-of-band and what is the most likely source of the discrepancy (model choice, stage mismatch, geography, or pricing page vs. realized ACV).
rule.rationale: Rewriting the plan conflates the market-researcher deliverable (distributions) with the product-manager deliverable (decisions). The researcher surfaces the gap; the founder owns the response.

rule.statement: Re-run the benchmark every 2 quarters. Flag any P50 metric that shifts more than 10% as a "regime change" and open an SDD task for the product-manager to revise the roadmap ARR targets.
rule.rationale: SaaS economics shift materially over 6-month periods in fast-moving categories. A benchmark that is 12 months old guides product and pricing decisions toward the wrong target distribution.

antipattern.description: Building a comp set with only public-listed companies
antipattern.reason: Survivorship bias: public companies run higher gross margin (78-82%) than venture-stage privates (60-70%) because of scale. Transplanting the median onto a $2M ARR target produces unachievable targets.

antipattern.description: Freemium conversion cited as "20%" without denominator disclosure
antipattern.reason: Companies cite conversion against activated or "qualified" users; system-wide conversion from signups is 1-4%. Always normalize to signups or the comparison is meaningless.

antipattern.description: NDR reported without gross logo retention
antipattern.reason: NDR above 110% coexists with gross logo churn above 25% in land-and-expand; the combination indicates an expansion-dependent model at high risk if new logo growth slows.

antipattern.description: Averaging cross-stage comps (seed + listed) without cohort annotation
antipattern.reason: A seed-stage Crunchbase pricing page averaged with a listed 10-K produces metrics that apply to neither stage. Cohort by stage before computing any statistic.

antipattern.description: Agent reads "annualized recurring revenue" and treats it as ARPU
antipattern.reason: ARR / ARPU / ACV are three different denominators. Require a glossary block in the output before any metric. Conflating them produces LTV calculations that are 10-500x off.

Shopify revenue is approximately 40% subscription, 40% payments (Merchant Solutions), and 20% other. Collapsing it to subscription distorts the subscription archetype's gross margin median by 5+ points.

antipattern.description: Agent reports marketplace headline commission (e.g., Etsy 6.5%) as the take rate
antipattern.reason: All-in take rate including seller subscriptions, payment fees, and ad revenue is 13-18% — 2x or more the headline commission. Headline commission misleads strategic pricing decisions.

antipattern.description: Agent uses today's pricing page against last year's filing metrics
antipattern.reason: SaaS pricing changes frequently; Wayback Machine snapshots at filing date are required to compare pricing to the financial metrics reported in the same period.

antipattern.description: Agent ships a verdict ("this model is viable") instead of a distribution
antipattern.reason: The market-researcher deliverable is distributions (P25/P50/P75). The verdict belongs to the researcher sibling and the product manager. Inverting this boundary mixes two distinct roles.

template.purpose: Business Model Canvas template with nine building blocks and revenue stream table
Unit economics calculator with LTV, CAC, payback, and break-even sections
Bash script emitting P25/P50/P75 per archetype from a comps.csv with a missing-N warning
